name: Fasting Glucose (FPG)
summary: Fasting glucose is the concentration of sugar in the blood after at least 8 hours without food or caloric drink. It is a core screening test for impaired glucose metabolism, prediabetes, and diabetes.
whatItMeans: A single fasting glucose value is a snapshot of how well the body keeps blood sugar in range between meals. By the American Diabetes Association, a fasting value of 70–99 mg/dL is considered normal, 100–125 mg/dL falls in the prediabetes (impaired fasting glucose) range, and 126 mg/dL or higher on two separate occasions is in the diabetes range. Higher fasting values generally reflect greater insulin resistance or reduced insulin output, while low fasting values may signal hypoglycemia. This is educational information, not a diagnosis.
raises: Excess body weight and abdominal adiposity
Diet high in refined carbohydrates and sugar
Acute stress, illness, or infection (stress hyperglycemia)
GH-axis peptides such as tesamorelin, somatropin, cjc-1295, and ipamorelin, which can worsen glucose tolerance
lowers: Weight loss and reduced calorie intake
Regular aerobic and resistance exercise (improved insulin sensitivity)
GLP-1 / GIP receptor agonists such as tirzepatide, retatrutide, and cagrilintide
Prolonged fasting or skipped meals (can drop into hypoglycemia)
confounders: Inadequate fasting (recent food or caloric drinks) falsely raises values
Acute illness, surgery, or stress transiently elevates glucose
Time of day and the 'dawn phenomenon' (morning cortisol/GH surge) can nudge values up
Delayed sample processing can lower measured glucose as red cells consume it (use a fluoride/oxalate tube)
Caffeine and some medications can affect readings

ranges.note: Normal fasting glucose 70–99 mg/dL; 100–125 mg/dL is prediabetes (impaired fasting glucose); ≥126 mg/dL on two occasions is in the diabetes range (American Diabetes Association).

references.title: Diabetes Diagnosis & Tests | American Diabetes Association
